perturbation.original_text: HDS will provide Celebrity with a one (1) year complimentary supply (i.e., at least 365 caplets) of Clotamin product during the Term.
perturbation.changed_text: HDS will provide Celebrity with a single complimentary supply (i.e., one caplet) of Clotamin product during the Term.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the complimentary supply of Clotamin to a single caplet could be argued as an unconscionable and commercially unreasonable consideration, potentially voiding the contract. This is inconsistent with general contract law principles that require adequate consideration.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-302 - Unconscionable Contract or Clause
perturbation.law_explanation: Modifying the consideration to be a single caplet could be deemed unconscionable under UCC § 2-302 because it is commercially unreasonable and shocks the conscience, potentially rendering the agreement unenforceable. This section allows a court to refuse to enforce a contract or clause found to be unconscionable at the time it was made.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. (1) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (2) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose and effect to aid the court in making the determination. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly reduces the provided supply from a year's worth to a single caplet, creating a potential issue of unconscionability as argued in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation. UCC § 2-302, as per Scraped Law Snippet 1, allows a court to refuse to enforce a contract if it finds any clause to be unconscionable at the time it was made. The drastic reduction in consideration could be viewed as commercially unreasonable, potentially leading to the agreement being deemed unenforceable under this section.

perturbation.original_text: Said written approval must be given within five (5) business days of CSE's receipt or said usage shall be deemed unapproved.
perturbation.changed_text: Said written approval must be given within one (1) business day of CSE's receipt or said usage shall be deemed unapproved.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the approval timeframe to one business day may be commercially unreasonable and insufficient for adequate review, particularly for complex advertising materials. This could potentially create a breach of contract issue if reasonable review is not possible.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Reasonable Time for Performance
perturbation.law_explanation: Reducing the approval time to one business day may violate the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, particularly if it is not a commercially reasonable time for review of the advertising materials. Section 204 of the Restatement (Second) of Contracts deals with supplying an omitted essential term when it's reasonable. Here, one business day for approval is potentially unreasonable.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the approval timeframe from five business days to one, which the Perturbation and Law Explanations suggest may violate the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing because it is not commercially reasonable. Although there are no scraped law snippets, the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition states that a contradiction arises when a contract sets a deadline or requirement that violates federal or state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable. Reducing the timeframe significantly could make adequate review impossible, leading to a potential breach and rendering the one-day requirement unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: HDS shall have the right to terminate this Agreement upon ten (10) days prior written notice to Celebrity in the event Celebrity fails to perform the duties set forth in Paragraph 4 hereof or breaches any other covenant or agreement set forth herein and fails to cure same (if curable) within seven (7) days of receipt of written notice.
perturbation.changed_text: HDS shall have the right to terminate this Agreement immediately upon written notice to Celebrity in the event Celebrity fails to perform the duties set forth in Paragraph 4 hereof or breaches any other covenant or agreement set forth herein.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the cure period for breaches and allowing immediate termination could be considered a penalty clause, especially if the breach is minor or easily curable. Penalty clauses are often unenforceable under contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Unenforceable Penalty Clause
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the cure period and allowing immediate termination, the modified clause may operate as an unenforceable penalty under Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 356. This section states that damages for breach may be liquidated in the agreement but only at an amount that is reasonable in light of the anticipated or actual loss caused by the breach, and that a term fixing unreasonably large liquidated damages is unenforceable on public policy grounds as a penalty.
perturbation.justification: The original contract provided a cure period for breaches, while the changed contract allows immediate termination. The Law Explanation, referencing Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 356, suggests this could be an unenforceable penalty, particularly if the breach is minor. Although the scraped law snippets are missing, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation detail how the removal of the cure period can act as a penalty, contradicting the principle that damages should be reasonable and not punitive, thereby creating a legal contradiction.
